food_subgroup: Boiled peanuts dry-tempered with South Indian spices

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Peanut Sundal. Nearest parent is a cooked legume snack preparation. A new concept for South Indian sundal preparations (boiled legumes dry-tempered with mustard seeds, curry leaves, and coconut) is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Cooked legume snack, South Indian tempered style (closest parent)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Peanut Sundal. A03RJ (cooked legumes) is used as the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Peanut Sundal as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Peanut Sundal is approximately 100 g (one small bowl), as commonly served at South Indian tiffin counters and as a festival prasad portion.

nutrient_source.title: Peanut Sundal — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, Entry EI000054
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from ICMR-NIN entry EI000054 for Peanut Sundal (Verkadalai Sundal). Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values are from enriched_json with confidence 0.9, consistent with USDA peanut composition data as a secondary cross-reference.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Peanuts — University of Sydney GI Database; corroborated by ICMR-NIN assessment
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 14 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for boiled/roasted peanuts (GI ~14), which is the primary ingredient of Peanut Sundal. The tempering ingredients (mustard seeds, urad dal, curry leaves, coconut) contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (14 × 16.5) / 100 = 2.31, rounded to 2.3 per 100 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 215 mg per 100 g; offset by high potassium (385 mg) and magnesium (52 mg)
hypertension.guidance: Peanut Sundal contains moderate sodium (215 mg/100 g) from added salt during tempering; however, its high potassium-to-sodium ratio and magnesium content support vascular tone, making it acceptable for hypertensive individuals when portion size is controlled and salt is minimised during preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low GI of 14; high protein (10.5 g), fat (17.5 g), and dietary fibre (5.2 g) per 100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of 14 and a glycemic load of approximately 2.3 per 100 g serving, Peanut Sundal produces a minimal postprandial glucose response and is well-suited for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed as part of a balanced diet.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero cholesterol; low saturated fat (3.8 g/100 g); high vitamin E (4.5 mg), folate (48 mcg), and magnesium (52 mg)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Peanut Sundal is cholesterol-free with a favourable unsaturated-to-saturated fat profile; its vitamin E content (4.5 mg/100 g) provides antioxidant protection, and its folate and magnesium levels support endothelial function, making it a heart-friendly snack in moderate portions.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Vitamin E (4.5 mg/100 g) and folate (48 mcg/100 g) support anti-inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Peanut Sundal provides vitamin E and folate, both relevant to immune modulation and anti-inflammatory pathways, and may be included in the diet of individuals with rheumatoid arthritis without concern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Peanut Sundal (Verkadalai Sundal) is a classic South Indian snack originating from Tamil Nadu, India. It is prepared by soaking raw peanuts (groundnuts) overnight and boiling them until tender, then dry-tempering them in a small amount of oil with mustard seeds, urad dal (split black gram), curry leaves, dried red chilli, and green chilli. The dish is finished with freshly grated coconut, which adds a subtle sweetness and texture contrast. The result is a dry, protein-rich snack with a nutty, mildly spiced flavour and a firm yet yielding bite. Peanut Sundal is a traditional Navratri festival offering and temple prasad in Tamil Nadu, and is widely served at South Indian restaurants and tiffin counters in Singapore as a starter or snack. Nutritionally, it provides 252 kcal, 10.5 g protein, 17.5 g fat, and 16.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, with 5.2 g dietary fibre and a very low glycemic index of 14, making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular risk. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from ICMR-NIN entry EI000054, the primary approved source for Indian dishes. GI value of 14 is consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database for peanuts. All core macros are complete and internally consistent. Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) are from enriched_json at confidence 0.9, cross-referenced with USDA peanut composition.
notes: Cholesterol is 0.0 mg — naturally zero as peanuts are a plant-based food containing no dietary cholesterol by nature. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 mcg — naturally zero as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients. Vitamin D is 0.0 mcg — naturally zero as peanuts and all tempering ingredients are plant-based with negligible vitamin D. Trans fat is 0.0 g — confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN source; no hydrogenated fats are used in traditional preparation. Monounsaturated fat and polyunsaturated fat are -1.0 as the ICMR-NIN source does not report these specific values. Water content is -1.0 as the ICMR-NIN source does not report this value. Portions in the input contained three duplicate entries for '1 serving (100g)'; deduplicated to a single default portion of 100 g. Copper (0.6 mg), manganese (1.1 mg), and vitamin K (0.2 mcg) are from enriched_json at confidence 0.9 and are consistent with USDA FoodData Central values for boiled peanuts. FoodOn URI is null as no specific concept exists for Peanut Sundal; closest parent is cooked legume snack. SNOMED CT code is null as no clinical food concept exists for this preparation.
